title: Our article among the most-viewed reviews in New Phytologist
content: Summary: Asteraceae represent one of the largest and most diverse families of plants. The evolutionary success of this family has largely been contributed to their unique inflorescences, capitula that mimic solitary flowers but are typically aggregates of multiple florets. Here, we summarize the recent molecular and genetic level studies that have promoted our understanding of the development and evolution of capitula. We focus on new results on patterning of the enlarged meristem resulting in the iconic phyllotactic arrangement of florets in Fibonacci numbers of spirals. We also summarize the current understanding of the genetic networks regulating the characteristic reproductive traits in the family such as floral dimorphism and differentiation of highly specialized floral organs. So far, developmental studies in Asteraceae are still limited to a very narrow selection of model species. Along with the recent advancements in genomics and phylogenomics, Asteraceae and its relatives provide an outstanding model clade for extended evo-devo studies to exploit the morphological diversity and the underlying molecular networks and to translate this knowledge to the breeding of the key crops in the family.

title: Fabio Tuccillolle maatalous- ja metsätieteellisen tiedekunnan väitöskirjapalkinto
content:
The Faculty of Agriculture and Forestry has awarded D.Sc. (Food Sc.) Fabio Tuccillo a dissertation prize of one thousand euros for his doctoral dissertation completed in 2025. The dissertation consists of three original articles and a summary. Faba bean is a protein-rich legume that is suitable for northern climates and has traditionally been cultivated in Finland, and in recent years, various meat-replacement products have been developed from them. However, the beany flavor and bitter taste of faba bean ingredients limit consumers acceptance of these products. 

In his dissertation, Fabio Tuccillo identified chemical compounds in faba bean flour, protein concentrates, and a protein isolate that elicit both positive and negative sensory perceptions, causing preferences and aversions, respectively. The work skillfully combined state-of-the-art chemical analytics and sensory evaluation, utilizing also artificial intelligence to predict the taste of the compounds based on their chemical structure.

title: Helsingin Yliopiston SMEAR II:lle ja Ilmatieteen laitoksen Pallaksen tutkimusasemalle ACTRIS laatusertifikaatit
content:
The University of Helsinki research station SMEAR II in Hyytiälä and the Pallas Atmosphere–Ecosystem Supersite by the Finnish Meteorological Institute have both been awarded the

The label was granted on 9 June 2026 by the ACTRIS ERIC General Assembly, which consists of representatives from ACTRIS member countries and formally approves facilities that meet strict operational and data-quality standards.

(Aerosol, Clouds and Trace Gases Research Infrastructure) is a pan-European organization that brings together leading atmospheric research facilities to produce high-quality observations of aerosols, clouds, and trace gases. These measurements are essential for understanding and addressing major societal and environmental challenges related to air quality, health, sustainability, and climate change. By providing a coordinated platform for accessing the research facilities and observational data, ACTRIS strengthens Europe’s capacity to advance atmospheric science and support evidence-based decision-making.

title: Tiedekulma lähtee Poriin – Helsingin yliopisto on monipuolisesti mukana SuomiAreenassa
content:
The University of Helsinki’s Think Corner is participating in SuomiAreena for the first time with its own programme. (in Finnish), offering the perfect chance to follow Ville Blåfield’s expert interviews over a morning coffee. Wednesday will focus on the treatment of obesity and overlooked medical conditions in women, with Thursday reserved for examining the global political situation. 

The expert interviews will feature Research Director Elisabeth Widén and Professor of Clinical Metabolism Kirsi Pietiläinen as well as Professor of Political History Juhana Aunesluoma and Postdoctoral Researcher in Social and Cultural Anthropology Annastiina Kallius of the University of Helsinki.

The University of Helsinki will join a notable group of partners in organising (in Finnish) on Wednesday, 24 June, from 10.00 to 14.00. The discussion will explore how Finnish food, including its production and food culture, can be turned into a stronger growth story, export product and source of national pride.

Details will be updated as the SuomiAreena programme is confirmed.

title: Save the date! Tiedolla vaikuttaminen ihmistieteissä -työpaja 12.8.
content:
Yliopistot, rahoittajat ja yhteiskunta vaativat enenevissä määrin tutkijoilta oman tutkimuksen hyödynnettävyyden ja vaikuttavuuden todentamista. Ihmistieteissä tämä vaade on erityisen vaikea sillä niiden tulokset ovat harvoin suoria ratkaisuja rajattuihin ongelmiin tai niiden vaikuttavuus selkeästi mitattavissa. Ihmistieteiden tutkijat ovat vaikuttavuusvaatimusten puristuksissa ilman kunnollisia työkaluja vastata tähän haasteeseen.

Työpajan ensimmäinen osa koostuu seminaarista, jossa keskitytään yhteiskunnalliseen vaikuttamiseen ihmistieteissä historiantutkijoiden kokemusten kautta. Toisessa osassa nostamme keskustelun yleisemmälle tasolle paneelikeskustelulla, jossa kysytään yliopiston ulkopuolisilta tiedon tuottamisen asiantuntijoilta parhaat yhteiskunnallisen vaikuttamisen keinot ihmistieteiden osaamista silmällä pitäen. Mitä tutkijat voivat oppia tiedolla vaikuttamisesta sen ammattilaisilta: ajatuspajoilta, tutkimuslaitoksilta ja viestintätoimistoilta?

Mitä? työpaja: seminaari + paneeli + ohjattuja harjoitteita + kahvi- ja kuohuviinitarjoilut

Missä? Helsingin yliopiston päärakennus, Fabianinkatu 33, 3. krs, sali F3020.

Milloin? 12.8.2026, klo 13–17

title: Tohtorikoulutushanke RESDOC tuo 30 kansainvälistä väitöskirjatutkijaa Helsingin yliopistoon ratkaisemaan kestävyyshaasteita
content:
The University of Helsinki has secured considerable EU funding to help build a resilient and just future through research. RESDOC seeks to train international researchers capable of developing such solutions across research, decision-making and business.

RESDOC is built around four broad research themes: infectious diseases and antimicrobial resistance in changing environment; biodiversity and nature’s resilience; safe and sustainable food and water systems; and just socio-ecological transition.

“RESDOC unites promising early-career researchers from across disciplines to investigate interconnected phenomena, including climate change, biodiversity loss, the spread of infectious diseases and the transformation of food and water systems. The goal is to equip them to become experts who not only produce new knowledge but also develop resilient and just solutions worldwide,” says RESDOC’s programme coordinator Minna Palander-Collin, Director of the University of Helsinki Doctoral School.

title: Looking back at HEPP6: Our biggest conference yet!
content:
The Sixth Helsinki Conference on Emotions, Populism, and Polarisation (HEPP6) broke new ground for HEPPsinki yet again. This time, we were thrilled to welcome 245 people from 47 countries and every continent except Antarctica to our hybrid event. We had 176 people with us onsite, where we were proud to hold the event for the first time in the . We are looking forward to meeting again at HEPP7 14-16 June 2027! 

The conference drew its main theme of grievance politics from its supporting which closed HEPP6 with a keynote panel highlighting the exciting contributions of some of its key researchers. However, the conference also featured several thematic streams running through the panel sessions, such as gender or memory politics, which our participants enthusiastically complimented. And, as always, HEPP6 featured numerous and varied contributions about populism theory, polarisation from myriad perspectives, and how emotions intertwine with media and politics.

title: Esko Karvinen will defend his PhD thesis
content:
Esko Karvinen will defend his doctoral thesis entitled “Biogenic carbon cycling dynamics in diverse urban green spaces” at the University of Helsinki on June 16, 2026 at 12.00.

The public examination will take place in auditorium 108 (B3) at the Forest Sciences Building, University of Helsinki (Latokartanonkaari 7, Helsinki). The Opponent is Associate Professor Andreas Ibrom, Technical University of Denmark, and the Custos is professor Jaana Bäck.

The book is available in Helda:

title: HIKET workshop brought together Finnish and Swedish researchers and experts in Helsinki
content:
A big thank you to all participants for the engaging conversations and valuable insights! We explored current developments, challenges and opportunities related to land-use carbon balance monitoring, greenhouse gas inventories, modelling and data integration.

The discussions highlighted the value of bringing together different perspectives and expertise to address the complex scientific questions surrounding land-use sector carbon balances.

The conversations don't end here - we look forward to continuing the collaboration and exploring new opportunities to work together in the future.

title: Arkadiankadun siltaremontti aiheuttaa muutoksia liikennejärjestelyihin Luonnontieteellisen museon lähistöllä
content:
The renovation will start with the construction of temporary traffic arrangements from Wednesday 7 January onwards. The renovation is expected to be completed in late 2026.

A temporary bridge for pedestrians will be built on the east side of Arkadiankatu over the Baana. The aim is to maintain the pedestrian route on the east side of the bridge until the temporary bridge is operational. Cyclists will also be able to use the bridge.

The Baana route underneath the bridge is expected to remain open for most of the renovation's duration, with some minor exceptions.

There will be changes to the motor vehicle routes by the bridge and around it in January. If possible, should be favoured during the bridge renovation.

Connections to the bus terminals in Kamppi will remain available, but there will be a slight diversion of routes.

Trams 1 and 1T will run via Simonkatu and Kamppi. Lasipalatsi and the Natural History Museum will not be served.

The diversion is due to the renovation of the Arkadiankatu bridge and will remain in place until late 2026. The renovation will not affect HSL's bus services. 

Pohjoinen Rautatiekatu 13

title: Ukrainan sota - yliopiston suositukset
content:
The University of Helsinki has condemned the military action by Russia and expressed its support for Ukraine and its university community. We have also expressed our support for Ukraine, together with other Finnish universities, through statements by and the . As a demonstration of support, we have illuminated buildings in the colours of the Ukrainian flag and raised the flag on our premises.

One of the principles of the University of Helsinki is to promote international collaboration in research and higher education, and to support these efforts even in difficult circumstances. Russia’s indefensible military aggression in Ukraine has required exceptional measures, which include those taken in the collaboration between universities.

The University of Helsinki is monitoring both national and EU-level sanctions, adapting our policies and processes accordingly. Together with other Finnish and international universities, the University of Helsinki strives to promote the end of military action in Ukraine, while supporting Ukrainian institutions and people.

title: 28.05.2026 ARGIH Talk: Maxim Demin
content:
On 28 May 2026, ARGIH held a seminar with Maxim Demin, a research fellow at Ruhr University Bochum in Germany, for a discussion on the relationship between free will and determinism in late imperial Russian thought. Drawing on research from his forthcoming book project, Experiments on the Soul: Philosophy and the Rise of Psychology in Imperial Russia, Demin explored how Russian intellectuals grappled with fundamental questions about human nature, moral responsibility, and scientific knowledge during a period of profound social and political transformation in the 1860s. 

Demin's primary field of research is post-Hegelian philosophy and its intellectual development in the German-speaking world during the nineteenth century. His interest in Russian intellectual history emerged naturally from his earlier work on the transformation of philosophy into a modern academic discipline. The rise of psychology and empirical research into consciousness, morality, and behaviour raised profound questions about the relationship between science and philosophy. 

When he turned to the Russian case, he was struck by how deeply Russian scholars and public intellectuals engaged with these debates and how closely connected they were to intellectual developments occurring across Europe. Rather than existing on the margins of European thought, Russian thinkers actively participated in transnational discussions about the nature of mind, morality, and scientific knowledge.
